generation tasks license: sam license (gated model on huggingface hub)", "the eugenia concept: from geometric feature extraction to associative knowledge assessment of a deterministic data analysis system mathematical foundation: geometric feature extraction via delta field and threshold sweep the eugenia project concept represents a holistic system for geometric and topological data analysis, built upon four key components: the mathematical foundation of delta field and threshold sweep, the pattern-graph architecture, radical compression methods via svd, and a universal geometric classifier. this section provides a deep analysis of the mathematical foundation the starting point of the entire system. it addresses how arbitrary input data is transformed into a unified, deterministic geometric profile that serves as the basis for all subsequent operations. the analysis focuses on two key elements: the delta field transformation and the threshold sweep algorithm, including jump detection mechanisms. the central element of geometric extraction is the transformation of input data into a delta field 17 . for images represented as pixel intensity matrices x with values in the range 0, 255 , this transformation is defined as: d log(x 1) - log(256 - x) this expression has important mathematical properties. the range of d values lies approximately within -5.545177, 5.545177 17 . the minimum value is achieved at x 0 ( log(1) - log(256) ), the maximum at x 255 ( log(256) - log(1) ), and the value 0 corresponds to mid-gray ( x approx 127.5 ) 17 . the derivative of this transformation, frac dd dx frac 1 x 1 frac 1 256-x , shows that the transformation is highly nonlinear: it stretches the dynamic range near the boundaries (transition from black to background or white to background), where the derivative is maximal ( 1), and compresses it in the central region (around gray), where the derivative is minimal ( 0.0159) 17 . this feature is fundamental: it converts smooth intensity changes into discrete, topologically significant events, making transitions between objects and background more pronounced and easily detectable 17 . thus, delta field acts not merely as a normalizing transformation, but as a tool for enhancing contrast at boundaries critical for subsequent topological analysis. after obtaining the field d , the eugenia system applies a threshold sweep algorithm. this involves systematically scanning the entire range of d values with a very small step, e.g., 0.0001, resulting in approximately 111,000 iterations to fully cover the range 17 . at each iteration corresponding to threshold tau , the field d is binarized: a mask (d tau) is created, representing a binary image where pixels with d values exceeding the current threshold are active. simultaneously, the occupancy value the fraction of active pixels in this binary slice, i.e., the mean of the mask is calculated 17 . this dense sequence of slices, from a completely black image ( tau to -5.545 ) to completely white ( tau to 5.545 ), provides a complete picture of how the image structure evolves as it is brightened. the key mechanism for extracting topological information from this sequence is jump detection. a jump event is recorded when the absolute change in occupancy between two adjacent thresholds exceeds a predefined threshold level, defaulting to 1 17 . mathematically: text occupancy ( tau) - text occupancy ( tau - delta tau) text jump threshold . each such jump represents a qualitative change in image connectivity. for example, a large positive jump may indicate that a new large object has been opened, while a large negative jump may indicate that an object is beginning to be closed by another object or disappearing from the image. these jump points are critical events encoding information about the appearance disappearance of connected components and the closing opening of cycles (holes) in the image 33,165 . thus, the entire threshold sweep process implements the core idea of filtration central to topological data analysis (tda) 65,67 . instead of analyzing the raw image directly, the system creates a sequence of its binary representations (a filtration) and then analyzes how its topology changes across this sequence. unlike classical tda approaches that often require explicit construction of complex mathematical structures like simplicial complexes (e.g., using gudhi or ripser libraries) 7,147 , eugenia offers a more heuristic but computationally efficient way to obtain similar information. from this process, a geometric profile a 144-dimensional vector serving as a summary characteristic of the image is extracted. this profile consists of several components: a discretized occupancy curve (binaryhistogram, 64-dimensional), a list of jump events (jumpevents, 20 elements, each containing threshold, previous and new occupancy values, and change magnitude), an approximation of betti numbers (bettisignature, 32-dimensional), and scalar characteristics such as capacity and phase signature 17 . this profile is then passed to the system for classification or storage in the pattern graph. importantly, the calculation of betti numbers within eugenia is simplified compared to formal persistent homology. from the provided materials, it appears to be implemented heuristically: betti-0 (number of connected components) and betti-1 (number of cycles or holes ) are counted on each binary slice of the mask, and these values are recorded in the signature 17 . this makes it computationally cheap but less rigorous and stable compared to formal persistent homology computation. theoretical results show that betti sequences can be unstable to small data perturbations 68,246 , although powerful stability theorems exist for persistence diagrams and barcodes 161,252 . the practical stability and reliability of the eugenia system will directly depend on proper selection of sweep parameters, such as step size and jump threshold. incorrect parameter choices may either miss important topological events or generate excessive false signals 5,6 . nevertheless, the successful demonstration on the mnist dataset achieving 100 accuracy on 10 samples (one per digit class 0-9) confirms that even these simplified topological invariants are sufficiently informative for separating simple classes 17 . the system effectively extracts the geometry of an object, which is less sensitive to exact pixel values than features obtained by standard convolutional neural networks, particularly important for few-shot tasks. pattern-graph architecture: associative storage and semantic search after the geometric profile (144-dimensional vector) is extracted from input data via delta field and threshold sweep, the next step in eugenia's architecture is its organization and storage. instead of using a simple vector store requiring exact matching or distance computation against all known samples, eugenia employs a more sophisticated structure: the pattern graph. this graph functions as a form of associative memory, enabling not only storage of reference profiles but also construction of semantic connections between them, significantly enhancing search and classification capabilities 179 . this section details the pattern-graph architecture, its key mechanisms such as and , and analyzes its potential and limitations. the pattern-graph structure is based on nodes and edges. each node represents a unique semantic pattern, an instance of the class 17 . such a pattern contains not only the geometric profile itself ( ), but also important meta the dominant singular value ( ), reflecting the pattern's importance or scale; a measure of information capacity or spectral entropy ( ); initial phase ( ); a usage counter ( ) that increments upon access; and a list of connected nodes with edge weights ( ) 17 . thus, each node is not merely a static vector, but a rich object containing information about the pattern itself, its context, and frequency of use. graph edges connecting nodes represent semantic relationships between patterns. edge weights typically equal the cosine similarity between the vectors represented by those nodes and lie in the range 0, 1 17 . the key mechanism enabling the graph to self-organize and enrich itself is the function. when a new pattern is extracted from input data, it is not simply added to the graph as an isolated node. instead, the system compares its vector profile against vectors of all existing nodes in the graph. if the cosine similarity between the new pattern's profile and an existing node's profile exceeds a predefined threshold (e.g., 0.75 0.85), the system automatically creates an edge connecting the two nodes 17 . this mechanism ensures self-organization: patterns most similar in geometry and topology become connected, forming clusters of semantically related concepts. this allows the graph to evolve from a simple list of references into a complex knowledge network where connections encode similarity or association relationships. threshold selection is critical: too low a threshold may create noisy, incorrect connections; too high may result in a fragmented graph where many meaningful connections are missed. another important function is . this mechanism tracks usage of each pattern. each time a pattern is used to respond to a query, its usage counter ( ) increments 17 . this may influence its weight or priority during search, making frequently accessed and relevant patterns more prominent in system reasoning. this adds another layer of dynamics and adaptivity to the graph's operation. search in such a graph is performed not by exact matching, but via the mechanism. upon receiving a query (a geometric profile), the system first finds the node in the graph most similar by vector. then, instead of stopping, it initiates a multi-hop graph traversal (e.g., using breadth-first search) from this initial node 17 . the search propagates to neighboring nodes (1st level), then to neighbors of neighbors (2nd level), and so on. edge weights along paths are aggregated (exact aggregation logic is unclear from sources), and the system returns the top-k most probable nodes found at the specified depth 17 . this mechanism enables discovery of patterns indirectly related to the query through chains of associations. for example, if the system encounters a previously unseen symbol, it may find its nearest neighbor in the graph and then use to search for other patterns also connected to that neighbor, thereby expanding the search scope and improving prediction reliability. eugenia's pattern-graph architecture closely aligns with concepts used in graph neural networks (gnns) and topological deep learning (tdl) 2,3 . gnns use graph structures to model interactions between objects, and their effectiveness largely depends on nodes' ability to aggregate information from neighbors 127 . in this sense, eugenia's pattern graph performs a similar function, but in the context of associative memory. however, despite obvious potential, there are limitations. the primary one is scalability. with millions of patterns, the graph can become extremely large and deep, making search slow. while the provided materials contain no information about using specialized search indices like hnsw or faiss widely employed for large vector spaces 130 this is an obvious direction for further development and scaling. another limitation is connection stability. the entire graph's quality directly depends on the correctness of the mechanism. as noted, threshold selection is critical, and its tuning may require significant effort for different datasets. despite these limitations, the pattern-graph architecture's potential is enormous. it opens the path to systems capable of not just classification, but more complex forms of reasoning based on connections. a vivid example of this potential is the described integration with external llms, such as gemma, in 17 . in this scenario, llm outputs (text) are first converted into a geometric profile, which is then added to the pattern graph. the mechanism connects this textual pattern with existing patterns in the graph (e.g., images or other text). as a result, the system enriches its internal knowledge model based on external sources, and upon subsequent queries, can return expanded context including both semantically close text fragments and related images or other data 17 . this demonstrates a transition from a static knowledge base to a dynamically evolving graph a powerful capability for building complex ai systems. radical compression via svd: from neural network weights to structural patterns one of the boldest and most original aspects of the eugenia concept is its approach to model compression, claimed in documentation as enabling compression of weight matrices totaling 111 gb down to approximately 1 gb using truncated svd (singular value decomposition) with parameter k 4..8 17 . this approach differs radically from traditional compression methods like quantization (fp16, int8) and requires rethinking the goal of compression. this section analyzes in detail how this mechanism works, what trade-offs it entails, and how it aligns with the system's overall deterministic paradigm as described in the module. the compression method's foundation is the well-known mathematical technique of truncated svd. any weight matrix w of size m times n can be decomposed into three matrices: w u sigma v t , where u is the matrix of left singular vectors of size m times r , sigma is the diagonal matrix of singular values of size r times r , and v t is the matrix of right singular vectors of size r times n , with r min(m, n) . many neural network weight matrices have low-rank structure, meaning their singular values decay rapidly. this allows approximate representation of the original matrix wk using only the first k columns of u and v t and the first k singular values in sigma , while retaining most of the matrix's energy (information). the truncated representation's size is m cdot k k k cdot n , which for small k values (e.g., k 4 or k 8 ) yields colossal savings compared to the original size m cdot n 38,117 . method compression ratio reconstruction error (frobenius) final size -------- ----------------- ---------------------------------- ------------ fp16 2 0 55.5 gb int8 4 1 27.8 gb svd k 4 125 99 0.89 gb svd k 8 62 98 1.78 gb int8 zlib 5 1 23 gb table compiled from data in . as shown, truncated svd with k 4 achieves a compression ratio of 125 , resulting in a size of just 0.89 gb 17 . however, this comes with a huge weight reconstruction error by frobenius norm, approximately 99 . here lies the key distinction of eugenia's approach. documentation explicitly states: reconstruction error... is not classification error 17 . the system abandons the goal of exact weight reproduction. instead, it preserves dominant singular directions represented by the first k columns of u and v t . these directions encode the most significant patterns and structures in the data that the model learned. fine-scale weights discarded during truncation are treated as noise not affecting final semantic classification. thus, there is a shift from representing data as a set of exact numbers to representing data as a set of dominant geometric and structural patterns. this approach aligns perfectly with eugenia's deterministic paradigm implemented in the kernel. after pattern extraction via svd, they are not used for weight updates as in traditional training. instead, they are stored as fixed, static objects 17 . each pattern is characterized by its vector, singular value, capacity, and phase. the forward pass through the system reduces to a purely algebraic operation: projecting the input vector onto the dominant singular direction, scaled by its importance 17 . the function looks like: 17 . this is a fully linear, reversible operation in the subspace spanned by the pattern vector. crucially, all these operations are deterministic: with fixed input and fixed patterns, the result is always identical. this is confirmed by the function, which runs the forward pass 100 times on the same input and verifies that the maximum difference between results is less than 1e-10 17 . additionally, the entire model is signed using sha256 of serialized patterns, providing a unique model identifier and ensuring that any change in patterns immediately reflects in the signature 17 . thus, radical compression via svd in eugenia is not merely a technical optimization, but a fundamental methodological choice. it enables a transition from the black box of deep learning, where weights are merely an intermediate stage, to creating a white box a universal knowledge map 17 . this knowledge map is not weights, but a fixed function that maps any input to a semantic pattern. this map's size is minimal ( 50 mb), yet it contains all necessary information for semantic analysis and data comparison 17 . this approach offers several important advantages. first, it ensures interpretability. instead of opaque weights, the model operates on a set of dominant singular vectors, each interpretable as a specific concept or theme the model extracted from data. second, it opens the path to deploying complex models on resource-constrained devices, as memory requirements drop by orders of magnitude. third, it promotes explainability of systems, critical for applications in scientific research, medicine, and other sensitive domains 118,181 . however, this approach has substantial trade-offs. the primary risk is loss of important information. discarding 98-99 of the weight matrix's energy 17 , the system risks missing subtle but important data details. this may render it ineffective for tasks requiring high precision, such as scientific computing or medical diagnostics. nevertheless, for tasks where data structure and geometry matter more than exact activation values, such compression levels are deemed acceptable. this aligns well with the universal knowledge map idea, which focuses on semantic patterns rather than weights 17 . future development in this direction may involve more sophisticated compression strategies, such as cross-layer correlation filtering described in and , which eliminates redundancy between different model layers before applying svd, enabling further size reduction without significant quality loss 17 . universal geometric classifier: few-shot operation the final and most practical component demonstrating eugenia's entire system potential is the universal geometric classifier, implemented in the module 17 . this classifier is not a traditional ml model trained on large datasets. instead, it embodies metric learning principles and operates in a few-shot learning regime, making it highly effective for a wide range of applications where collecting extensive annotated datasets is difficult. this section analyzes the architecture, operation, and potential of this classifier. the eugenia classifier's operating principle is based on a simple but powerful idea: any input object, after transformation into a geometric profile, should be matched with the reference sample most similar in geometry and topology. model training ( ) in this context does not mean weight updates. it reduces to a single operation: for each class represented in the training set , its geometric profile is extracted (via the already-described delta field and threshold sweep), and this profile is saved as the class representative 17 . notably, training requires only 1-10 examples per class, enabling the few-shot learning regime 17 . storing one class's profile occupies very little space approximately 2-5 kb demonstrating the approach's high efficiency 17 . the prediction process ( ) is the inverse operation. for a new, unfamiliar object , its geometric profile is first extracted. then this profile is sequentially compared against all saved class profiles. the key element here is the combined similarity metric, which does not rely on a single feature but aggregates information from various aspects of the geometric profile. documentation specifies the following formula for calculating similarity score: this formula shows the system assigns greatest weight to occupancy histogram similarity (40 ), then topological invariant similarity (betti signature, 30 ), jump event similarity (20 ), and finally scalar capacity characteristic similarity (10 ) 17 . such a weighted approach yields a more robust and reliable assessment, as different profile components encode different information about object structure. for example, the occupancy histogram reflects the object's overall density, the betti signature its topological complexity (component and hole counts), and jump events key moments of its formation. the prediction becomes the class whose profile showed the highest similarity score with the input object's profile. one of the classifier's most significant claims is its universality. documentation explicitly states it can work with any data type images, text, or audio provided they are pre-normalized to the 0, 1 range 17 . this indicates the delta field and threshold sweep concept is general enough to apply to various data formats. for example, text can be represented as a term-document frequency matrix, audio as a spectrogram. transformation into a delta field and subsequent sweep would allow extraction of geometric and topological features from these data, which the same classifier could then process. this opens broad horizons for eugenia's application in multimodal tasks. however, despite obvious advantages, this approach has limitations. first and foremost is dependence on geometric profile quality. classification accuracy directly depends on the informativeness and stability of features extracted by delta field and threshold sweep. as noted earlier, betti number calculation is simplified and may be sensitive to noise and sweep parameters 17 . improperly tuned parameters may lead to uninformative or noisy profiles, reducing classifier accuracy. a second limitation is potential non-invariance to geometric transformations. a standard implementation of such a classifier would be sensitive to rotations, scaling, translations, and other input data transformations. for example, a rotated digit may have a completely different geometric profile than the original and fail to be recognized. for real-world applications, either preliminary data normalization (e.g., centering and scaling) or integration of mechanisms ensuring invariance to such transformations would be required. the universality concept requires these invariants to be either built into the profile extraction procedure itself or achieved through additional data transformations. despite these limitations, the universal geometric classifier's potential is enormous. its effectiveness in few-shot learning makes it an ideal solution for many current machine learning challenges, such as cross-domain classification where training and test data come from different distributions 222,302 . it can be used for rapid labeling of new categories, for diagnostic systems based on sensor signals where data collection is costly and labor-intensive, or for document analysis where semantically similar texts need to be quickly found. the successful demonstration on mnist achieving 100 accuracy on 10 samples (one per class) is a vivid confirmation of this approach's power for class separation tasks based on geometric structure 17 . this shows that for some tasks, there is no need for the complex hierarchical representations built by deep networks; a simple but informative geometric characteristic suffices. integration and deterministic paradigm: system synthesis to fully assess eugenia's concept potential, one must consider not only its individual components but also how they integrate into a unified, holistic system operating within a strict deterministic paradigm. the eugenia project represents not merely a set of tools, but a sequential data processing pipeline where each stage's output becomes the next stage's input. this synthesis is key to understanding why the system works as it does and what its fundamental advantages and disadvantages are. this section traces the complete data flow through the system and analyzes how determinism underpins its entire architecture. the complete data flow in the eugenia system can be represented as the following sequence of actions: 1. universal input normalizer: any data source (image, text, audio) is first normalized to a unified range, e.g., 0, 1 17 . this ensures input data unification for subsequent stages. 2. geometric extractor: normalized data is transformed into field via delta field ( ) 17 . then threshold sweep is performed across the entire range, enabling jump event detection and calculation of occupancy curves and betti number approximations. the output is a 144-dimensional geometric profile 17 . 3. deterministic knowledge core: this profile is passed to the kernel, where it is analyzed via truncated svd. dominant singular directions are extracted and encoded as semantic patterns ( ). each pattern contains a vector-profile, singular value, capacity, and phase 17 . crucially, this extraction is deterministic: with a fixed input profile, the result is always identical. 4. pattern graph engine: new patterns are either created or retrieved from existing ones. they are inserted into the pattern graph, where the mechanism automatically builds connections with semantically similar patterns based on cosine similarity. the graph becomes a dynamic knowledge base 17 . 5. compression layer: for space efficiency and performance, weight matrices used for pattern extraction may be radically compressed via truncated svd (k 4..8), preserving only dominant structural patterns 17 . 6. query classification: to respond to a query (classification), the system performs a search for the geometrically nearest profile in storage (whether pattern graph or simple class profile set) using the combined similarity metric 17 . this pipeline demonstrates a transition from learning as optimization to learning as indexing and retrieval. instead of updating weights via gradient descent, eugenia extracts fixed, deterministic representations (profiles and patterns) from data and organizes them in a structure for fast search. each stage is deterministic. there are no random initializations, no stochastic gradient descent, no state that mutates over time. all the system remembers is a set of patterns and a graph connecting them. this deterministic approach is a fundamental eugenia advantage. it guarantees full reproducibility: rerunning the system on the same input data yields an absolutely identical result. this is critically important for scientific research and any systems where explaining the reason for a decision is necessary 118 . unlike deep learning black boxes, where internal processes often remain opaque, every step in eugenia has a clear mathematical interpretation. one can precisely state which geometric and topological features served as the basis for classification. this significantly simplifies analysis, debugging, and builds trust in the system. another consequence of determinism is the ability to create a unique model signature. in the module, the entire model is represented as a set of patterns that are serialized and signed using sha256 17 . the resulting 16-character identifier is a kind of model fingerprint. any change in the data from which patterns were extracted, or in the extraction algorithm itself, immediately changes this identifier. this ensures strict model integrity control and allows tracking of any modifications. however, the deterministic paradigm also imposes limitations. abandoning gradient-based learning means the system cannot adapt to new data on the fly in the way traditional neural networks do. its learning is more a process of knowledge base enrichment. adapting to new data requires recalculation and updating of patterns and the graph, which may be computationally expensive. additionally, the system may be less flexible in capturing very complex, nonlinear dependencies that modern deep networks can learn thanks to their multi-level hierarchical representations. despite this, the synthesis of the entire eugenia system represents a powerful and coherent concept. it offers an alternative, deterministic, and geometrically motivated approach to data analysis. its strength lies in combining the simplicity and expressiveness of individual elements (delta field transformation, svd truncation) with the power of associative graph search. this approach does not seek to replace deep learning, but offers a solution for tasks where interpretability, reproducibility, efficiency under data scarcity (few-shot learning), and handling heterogeneous data types are important. final potential and development directions for eugenia the eugenia concept, presented as a working prototype, extends far beyond a simple set of data analysis tools. it represents a holistic, deterministic paradigm offering an alternative perspective on solving machine learning tasks. assessing its potential requires comprehensive analysis of its strengths, limitations, and paths for future development. eugenia is not a finished solution, but a verified foundation with high potential for scaling and generalization, as the user stated. its strength lies in the synergy of four key components: geometric feature extraction, associative graph storage, radical structural compression, and efficient few-shot classification. core strengths and unique advantages of eugenia: 1. determinism and reproducibility: this is the system's fundamental property. absence of randomness and state guarantees that for the same input, the result is always identical 118 . this radically distinguishes eugenia from stochastic deep learning models and makes it extremely attractive for scientific research, medical diagnostics, and other domains requiring explainability and predictability 181 . 2. high interpretability: every step in eugenia's pipeline has a clear mathematical interpretation. the user does not work with a black box. one can see exactly which geometric and topological features (occupancy curves, betti numbers, jumps) were extracted from data and how they influenced the final classification result. this significantly simplifies debugging, analysis, and builds trust in the system. 3. efficiency in few-shot learning regime: the system demonstrates outstanding performance where traditional deep learning methods require large data volumes. the ability to learn from 1-10 examples per class makes eugenia an ideal tool for tasks involving rare classes, rapid labeling of new categories, or cross-domain classification 13,17,218 . 4. universality and multimodality: the claimed ability to work with any data after simple normalization to is a huge advantage 17 . this opens the path to creating a unified platform for analyzing text, images, audio, and other data types using a single, geometrically motivated approach. 5. radical compression and compactness: the svd-based compression approach enables creation of compact knowledge maps sized in megabytes that contain all necessary information for semantic analysis 17 . this not only improves storage efficiency but also opens possibilities for deploying complex models on resource-constrained devices (edge computing). main limitations and areas for development: 1. theoretical rigor and stability: many key operations, especially betti number calculation, are used as heuristics rather than strictly defined mathematical operations from topological data analysis (tda) 17 . theoretical results show that some topological invariants may be unstable to small data perturbations 68,246 . to enhance system reliability and trust, deeper theoretical analysis of its stability is needed, and heuristics may need replacement with more rigorous mathematical analogs, such as formal persistent homology computation. 2. parameter sensitivity: system performance, like many heuristic methods, heavily depends on correct parameter selection: sweep step, jump detection thresholds, graph connection thresholds 17 . automating this tuning process (e.g., via meta-learning or adaptive algorithms) is an important direction for further work. 3. graph scalability: with large data volumes, the pattern graph can become too large and deep, slowing search. efficient graph indexing and search methods (e.g., hnsw, faiss) need to be implemented to ensure fast system response with millions of nodes 130 . 4. geometric invariance: the standard implementation likely lacks sufficient invariance to rotations, scaling, and other geometric transformations. to enhance universality and real-world applicability, invariance must either be built into the profile extraction algorithm itself or achieved via preliminary data normalization. 5. limited model complexity: the deterministic nature and abandonment of gradient learning limit the system's ability to capture very complex, hierarchical, and nonlinear dependencies that modern deep networks can learn. eugenia excels at tasks based on geometry and topology but may underperform on tasks requiring multi-level abstract reasoning. directions for future development: hybridization: the most promising direction is not opposing eugenia to deep learning, but integrating them. for example, eugenia could extract highly informative geometric and topological features from data, which are then fed into a small neural network for final classification. this would combine eugenia's deterministic interpretability with deep models' flexibility and power. enhancing theoretical rigor: transitioning from heuristics to formal tda methods like persistent homology could enhance system reliability and stability, making it more applicable to scientific tasks 7,147 . automation and adaptation: developing algorithms for automatic tuning of key parameters (sweep step, thresholds) based on input data properties. expanding graph functionality: implementing mechanisms for working with temporal data (dynamic graphs), integration with external apis for knowledge base enrichment, and improving semantic search algorithms. user tool development: creating convenient interfaces for visualizing the pattern graph, analyzing individual profiles, and interpreting classification results. in conclusion, the eugenia concept represents a powerful and verified foundation with enormous potential. it offers a viable alternative to traditional machine learning approaches, especially in tasks where interpretability, reproducibility, and efficiency with limited data are important. while achieving commercial maturity and broader application requires addressing current limitations, its fundamental idea creating a deterministic, geometrically motivated system for data analysis is promising and deserves further study and development."]

Name: 3_transformers_library_transformers version_model
Representative_Docs: ["한국어 버전(펼치지 접기) 자세한 설명 감사합니다 두 모델이 비전 인코더, lm 백본, vl 커넥터 측면에서 상당한 차이를 보이는 것은 사실이지만, transformers 아키텍처의 유연성 덕분에 44314의 기존 작업으로 도 충분히 커버할 수 있다고 생각합니다. 3b 모델 역시 llava 기반인 만큼, 제 pr의 과 으로 지원이 가능할 것으로 보입니다. 구현상 추가가 필요한 부분은 크게 두 가지입니다: 1. mm projector: 현재 seed-32b 모델이 단순 linear 레이어를 사용하고 있어 구현이 최소화된 상태입니다. 다만 초기 코드베이스에는 gemma3나 qwen과 유사한 형태의 ( 서브클래스)가 이미 포함되어 있었으며, 이를 재도입하면 3b 모델에서 사용하는 c-abstractor를 지원할 수 있습니다. - 참고: modelinghyperclovaxvision.py l604-l746 ( 2. image processor: seed-32b는 qwen의 이미지 처리 파이프라인을 사용하기 때문에 별도의 image processor가 제거된 상태입니다. 하지만 초기 커밋에는 anyres를 지원하는 가 포함되어 있었으며, 이는 3b 모델의 siglip 기반 파이프라인과 자연스럽게 연결될 수 있습니다. - 참고: imageprocessinghyperclovaxvision.py ( 한 가지 짚고 넘어갈 점은, v1과 v2의 weight map이 서로 다르기 때문에 에서 두 경우를 각각 처리하는 조건 분기가 필요하다는 것입니다. 이는 충분히 대응 가능한 작업이지만, 진행 전에 리뷰어와 먼저 방향을 맞춰보고 싶습니다. 다만 이 문제는 근본적으로 두 모델이 동일한 을 공유하면서 동일한 을 사용하게 되는 데서 비롯됩니다. 께서 제안하신 것처럼, 제 pr의 을 로, 를 으로 각각 분리하는 방법도 고려해볼 수 있을 것 같습니다. 이렇게 하면 각 버전에 대한 독립적인 을 유지하면서도, v1과 v2가 가능한 한 동일한 코드 경로를 공유하도록 구성하는 것이 가능할 듯합니다. 전반적인 의견: ( 44956)와 ( 44314)에 대해 별도의 modeling 파일을 유지하기보다는, gemma3 패턴처럼 하나의 modeling 파일 안에서 과 을 모두 지원하는 방향으로 통합하는 것이 더 깔끔하고, transformers가 모듈화 중심으로 나아가고 있는 방향과도 잘 맞는다고 생각합니다. 이를 통해 불필요한 코드 중복을 피하고 유지보수 부담도 줄일 수 있습니다. 다만 이 부분은 결국 리뷰어의 판단이 가장 중요합니다. -nlp 앞으로 이 pr들을 어떻게 조율해 나갈지 방향을 잡아주시면 감사하겠습니다. thanks for the detailed breakdown while the two models do differ significantly in their vision encoder, lm backbone, and vl connector, i believe the existing work in 44314 can actually cover as well, thanks to the flexibility of the transformers architecture. since the 3b model is also llava-based, and in my pr should be sufficient to support it. the main implementation delta boils down to two things: 1. mm projector: the seed-32b model uses a plain linear layer, which is why the current implementation is minimal. that said, the original codebase already included a full ( subclass) similar to what you'd see in gemma3 or qwen which can be reintroduced to support the c-abstractor used in the 3b model. - reference: modelinghyperclovaxvision.py l604-l746 ( 2. image processor: the seed-32b relies on qwen's image processing pipeline, which is why a standalone image processor was dropped. however, the initial commit did include a full with anyres support, which should map cleanly to the 3b model's siglip-based pipeline. - reference: imageprocessinghyperclovaxvision.py ( one thing worth noting is that the weight maps between v1 and v2 differ, so would need conditional branching to handle both cases correctly. that's a manageable addition, but something i'd want to align on with the reviewers before proceeding. that said, this issue fundamentally stems from both models sharing the same and thus the same . as suggested, one viable approach would be to explicitly separate them assigning to my pr and to . this would allow each version to maintain its own independent , while still enabling v1 and v2 to share as much of the underlying code as possible. my overall take: rather than maintaining separate modeling files for ( 44956) and ( 44314), it might be cleaner and more in line with how transformers is evolving toward modular management to consolidate into a single modeling file that supports both and , similar to the gemma3 pattern. this would avoid unnecessary code duplication and reduce maintenance overhead. that said, this is ultimately a call for the reviewers to make. -nlp would appreciate some guidance on how you'd like to see these prs coordinated going forward.", 'title: clarification and consistency of transformers terminology in readme while reading the readme as a new user, i found the use of the term transformers slightly ambiguous. at first glance it can refer either to the transformer architecture or to the transformers python library. for example, the sentence: transformers acts as the model-definition framework for state-of-the-art machine learning... appears to refer to the library, but the wording initially made it unclear whether it referred to the architecture or the transformers library. additionally, the readme sometimes uses: - transformers - transformers (the python package) - transformers which may be slightly confusing for beginners encountering the project for the first time. suggestion: would it make sense to clarify references to the library in places where it may be ambiguous, for example by writing: the transformers library or using the existing project style such as transformers ? i would be happy to open a small documentation pr once the preferred wording style is confirmed.', 'transformers 5.5.3']

Name: 4_model_remote code_remote_models
Representative_Docs: ['hey all! indeed as answered, if you want to avoid reinit, you can simply override in your model, and make it do nothing! you can also check my answer here ( - we may bring that back as default for remote code!', 'it sounds weird but some of the vllm ci is breaking because we deleted . but we have something like a hack to import that return the one from . but for remote code when you reload the codebase or you are still trying to access it (remote code that imports in a weird way). this could help for our remote code compat', "generation config merging logic prevents explicit override of model-specific defaults back to global defaults generation config merging logic prevents explicit override of model-specific defaults back to global defaults problem summary the current generation config merging logic in makes it impossible to explicitly override a model's generation config parameter back to the global default value. this breaks the principle of explicit parameter passing and creates surprising behavior. minimal reproduction root cause the merging logic in : logic: if custom value equals global default and model has a different value, assume user didn't explicitly set it and use model's value. problem: cannot distinguish between: - user explicitly sets (should be respected) - user accepts default (could use model's value) why this is wrong 1. violates principle of explicit parameters 2. makes override impossible - if model has and you want global default - setting doesn't work (gets overwritten) - have to use hacky workarounds: we are proposing to temporarily overwrite model generation config and then restore its original value 3. no escape hatch - no way to say i really mean 1.0, don't override it - no flag or similar 4. surprising behavior - most users don't expect hidden merging - explicit parameter passing should be transparent - a warning message is easy to miss ignore real-world impact this issue affects some trl's trainers (grpo, rloo,...) and likely other training libraries: - grpo training requires for proper variance in rewards - qwen models have in their - setting via gets silently overwritten to - result: zero variance in rewards, grpo training fails workaround in trl: have to directly modify to avoid the merging logic, which has its own problems (changes the generation config file of the trained model pushed to hub). environment - transformers version: main branch - model: trl-internal-testing tiny-qwen25vlforconditionalgeneration (and any model with non-standard generation_config.json) - affected use cases: training libraries (trl, etc.), fine-tuning, custom generation additional context a comment describing a similar issue to the one reported here: - pr which introduced this merging logic: - 36684 related discussion in trl: - this issue makes it difficult to build training libraries that need different generation parameters than the model's inference defaults. the current workaround (modifying directly) has side effects. cc: (who proposed this merging logic in 36684), -nlp (who reviewed it), and (who also commented the pr)"]

Name: 5_weights_weight_checkpoint_tied
Representative_Docs: ["hey -d! it's indeed the current intended behaviour, and we are aware that it's bc-breaking sometimes, that's why you now see a warning in such cases! the problem here lies in the fact that due to another previous silent bug, some models did not have their embeddings tied by default, even though the default config field is . after we refactored the logic, this silent bug disappeared (that's what happen when you make things simpler and more logical ). but the models that were previously victims of this silent bug, for example some , and they naturally have 2 very different weights for each one. tying them anyway would completely destroy the model, as their weight are supposed to be different. for this reason, i introduced the check and the warning. i thought about adding an equality check on top, to tie both weights anyway if both matrices have the same values, but ultimately decided that this was an expensive check to perform (as embeddings can be very large), and not absolutely needed, as the warning is always displayed and we don't expect many models that have this issue. moreover, if both weights are present in the checkpoints, it means that the weights were not tied when saving the model (otherwise tied weights are removed from saved checkpoints), so it makes sense to not tie them back when loading.", "question about tieweights hi, i noticed that the logic of the tieweights function has changed in the transformers 5.0.0rc. in v4.x, when tiewordembeddings true, weights between embedtokens.weight and lmhead.weight were always tied, regardless of whether both tensors were present in the checkpoint. however, in v5.0.0rc, if both embedtokens.weight and lmhead.weight are explicitly present in the checkpoint, the model no longer ties them, resulting in two independent copies of the weights. this change affects training behavior: gradients will now be computed and applied separately to the two weight matrices, potentially leading to divergent updates and inconsistent model behavior compared to previous versions. i'm concerned this might introduce subtle training instabilities or break backward compatibility for users who expect tied embeddings and output layers by default (as was the case in v4.x). i m wondering whether this change could lead to any unexpected problems for example, although embedtokens.weight and lmhead. weight exist in the checkpoint, i still want to tie these two weights. thanks!", "bug: lmhead weight not tied in mistral3forconditionalgeneration (affects automodelforimagetexttotext) system info - version: 5.0.0.dev0 - platform: linux-5.15.133 -x8664-with-glibc2.35 - python version: 3.12.0 - pytorch version: 2.9.0 cu126 - cuda cudnn version: 12.6 - gpu: tesla t4 (compute capability 7.5) who can help? -nlp information - x the official example scripts - my own modified scripts tasks - x an officially supported task in the folder (such as glue squad, ...) - my own task or dataset (give details below) reproduction issue description when loading via for model , the parameter is not properly tied to input embeddings, causing random initialization and gibberish outputs. model loading warning code to reproduce actual output (gibberish) root cause the checkpoint file ( ) contains only and not , as these weights are meant to be tied (shared). the automatic weight tying mechanism in is not working. verification: workaround manually tying the weights fixes the issue: proposed fix add proper weight tying to : additional context - affects all ministral-3 models (3b, 8b, 14b variants) - breaks fine-tuning workflows as model starts with corrupted weights - model structure shows: - : shape properly loaded - : shape randomly initialized - these should be the same tensor (tied weights) expected behavior the model should: 1. automatically tie weights during loading: should reference 2. generate coherent text for the test prompt what is 2 2? - expected output: or similar coherent response - current output: gibberish (random tokens from corrupted lm_head) the weight tying should happen automatically in the model's method, similar to how other transformer models (bert, gpt-2, llama) handle this."]

Name: 8_tokenizer_tokenizers_token_bpe
Representative_Docs: ['tokenization breaking changes - slow tokenizer files (aka: ) will be removed in favor of using fast tokenizer files -- will be renamed to . as fast tokenizers are :hugs: - backend, they include a wider range of features that are maintainable and reliable. - other backends (sentence piece, tokenizers, etc.) will be supported with a light layer if loading a fast tokenizer fails - remove legacy files like specialtokensmap.json and addedtokens.json - remove eventuallycorrectt5maxlength - -- - -- (will continue to update this list) more info here: and here:', "so the issue seems to be that in 4.57.3 we just loaded the file which specifies the tokenizer . in v5 we seem to be just manually generating the tokenizer instead of trusting the file . and so we're overwriting the tokenizer for llama 3 . i'm not sure what's the correct fix but a hackish fix is to check for vocab and then create the tokenizers with respect to that . i have a implemented this fix but it does include iterating the vocab via itertools .", "hi, i think there are also other embeddia ( models affected by this issue, so this is not a problem of a single small model. i did some digging and found that the problem stems from model's tokenizer.json ( file: est-roberta ( (and other embeddia's models, such as embeddia litlat-bert ( and embeddia sloberta ( are declaring model's vocabulary as a dictionary, for instance: while the camemberttokenizer expects vocabulary as a list of tuples here ( which also gives the error. so, the first idea is that maybe the data conversion is incomplete; i've found a block of code in tokenizersbackend responsible for converting the vocabulary loaded from json ( and it does not expect dict format data. a simple correction to be added there would be: but i am not sure that it solves the whole problem. i experimented with it a bit and after converting vocab to a list of tuples, i got another error from the next line ( in camemberttokenizer: actually, the tokenizer type declared in estroberta's tokenizer.json ( is bpe, not unigram -- could that be part of the reason? anyway, i am only guessing here as i am not familiar with the tokenizers system. i need some feedback from the people who have developed the tokenizers loading code."]

Name: 10_v5_release_patch_v4
Representative_Docs: ['thanks for sharing the v5 plan and opening this thread. i have a few general questions that may be useful for end users and downstream maintainers: - is there a centralized, running list of proposed or accepted breaking changes for v5? - is there an index of rfcs or enhancement proposals, along with their discussion decision status? - what is the deprecation policy for v5 (and remaining v4 support), and what are the time windows for v5 rc testing and v4 deprecating? - will breaking changes in transformers require coordinated updates in other hugging face packages (e.g., , , , , etc.)? thanks again for all the work on the v5 transition.', "welcome v5 in this issue we share our plan for the upcoming version 5 of transformers. we've talked about version 5 for years and it's finally around the corner! we'll release a blog post announcing the focus of this release shortly, and wanted to share what we believe the process will look like over the coming weeks. - soon, a new branch named will be created on the repository. it is from this branch that all v4-related updates will take place. going forward, will act as the version 5 branch. - for the next few weeks, every pr except breaking changes or significant refactors will be merged in both and . - in a few weeks, we release what will likely be one of the last minor v4 releases ( ) - a few weeks later, we will release . we will aim to limit, as much as possible, the breaking changes within that release; but expect a migration guide as well as some specific breaking changes enabling much more versatile, performant, and cleaner code going forward. - over the next few months, we'll continue patching the branch and will release patch updates. the v5 on pypi will be preceded by rc releases that we will share in this issue. please subscribe to this issue to be updated, and let us know if you have thoughts about the outlined process above.", "will release a patch today we're only a very small team of maintainers, and all extremely busy these past weeks due to the incoming v5, sorry for the inconvenience"]

Representative_Docs: ["that would be a nice feature indeed! if it's something you, , or someone else can open a pr for, we'd be happy to provide feedback! as a pointer, whisper's generate method ( here ( would be the place to refer to for adding such functionality in a similar way, but not necessarily the only way", 'hi maintainers, thanks for the update, and great work on getting this implemented. i had independently built an implementation before noticing the issue was marked as completed. my work includes a working implementation, test coverage, and supporting examples, all ready for submission. i completely understand the feature is already complete, but if you are open to reviewing improvements, additional tests, documentation, or refactoring, i would be happy to open a pull request for consideration. please let me know if you d like me to submit it, and i can link the pr here.', "integration add pluto experiment tracker callback. feature request add as an option for natively logging to the pluto experiment tracker. motivation we recently launched a new oss experiment tracker ( client code ( server code ( and we're hoping to get integrated as a logging callback option for those using . we're currently offering a hosted version as well in the link below. hoping to hear back if it is okay if we open a pr for this. quite a few of our users moved from neptune given the shutdown so we want to close the gap so people have to time to try it out before the neptune shutdown date. your contribution i will be drafting the pr and managing it until it is merged."]

Name: 13_audio_inference time_voxtral_removed
Representative_Docs: ['audio weightnorm standardization !important draft: this issue is provided for visibility, the below recommendations will evolve this issue serves as a tracker to standardise the usage of weightnorm throughout the library for our audio models and establish good practices. different approaches 1. conversion time: remove weight norm once when converting the weights 2. inference time: remove weight norm at init, meaning the loaded stateddict is the one with weight norm weight norm is removed at init 3. do not remove weight norm, meaning the correct weight is recomputed each time a summary of how it is done currently throughout the lib: model in transformers original codebase source project --------------------- ---------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------ dac conversion time inference time (not removed) encodec inference time (not removed) inference time (not removed) fastspeech2conformer na (copied from) na (copied from) hubert inference time (not removed) inference time (not removed) mimi likely weights have been converted likely weights have been converted for the inference version seamlessm4t na (copied from) na (copied from) seamlessm4tv2 na (copied from) na (copied from) sew inference time (not removed) sewd inference time (not removed) speecht5 inference time (not removed) unispeech inference time (not removed) unispeechsat inference time (not removed) univnet conversion time vits inference time (not removed) wav2vec2 inference time (not removed) wav2vec2conformer inference time (not removed) wavlm inference time (not removed)', "ok tänks bro في السبت 25 أبريل 2026 19:59 travis briggs . كتب: audiodude created an issue (huggingface transformers 45647) full disclosure: this issue was found and written up by claude code system info - e 0, 0 .float()).abs().mean().item()print(f output diff (a vs eb): diff:.4f ) results transformers output diff (a vs eb) audio conditioning 4.48 0.1466 works 5.5.4 0.0000 ignored where the chain breaks (per v5.5.4 tracing) - Name: 15_loss_trainer_training_function
Representative_Docs: ['custom loss function computation even when training without labels. feature request currently, allows a custom function to be passed as a trainer argument. however, this user defined loss is only accessed in the case when labels is not none. i think that this hidden dependency is counter-intuitive. user defined loss functions should take priority regardless of context, and not be ignored without at least a warning. this logic can be seen here: motivation i find conditionally calling based on other configuration of the trainer to be odd and counter-intuitive. it leads to customizations being ignored in unpredictable ways. overall, if the user defines a loss function, they expect that function to be used, rather than ignored. therefore, the trainer kwarg should take first priority. your contribution user defined loss functions should come first i could implement such a change, if needed.', "at the moment, trainer.evaluate() only reports scalar metrics (loss, evalloss, etc.), but there is no built-in way to inspect the actual decoded predictions on the evaluation set. for text generation tasks, being able to log a few predicted samples during evaluation would be very helpful for monitoring qualitative progress. workarounds: subclass trainer and override evaluate() to decode predictions. use a custom computemetrics that prints decoded outputs. call trainer.predict() manually outside training. suggestion: introduce an optional argument (e.g., logtextsamples n) that automatically logs n decoded predictions alongside evaluation metrics. contribution: i d be happy to work on this feature and open a pr if it s considered useful. that'it, thank you in advance", 'at the moment, trainer.evaluate() only reports scalar metrics (loss, evalloss, etc.), but there is no built-in way to inspect the actual decoded predictions on the evaluation set. for text generation tasks, being able to log a few predicted samples during evaluation would be very helpful for monitoring qualitative progress. workarounds: subclass trainer and override evaluate() to decode predictions. use a custom computemetrics that prints decoded outputs. call trainer.predict() manually outside training. suggestion: introduce an optional argument (e.g., logtextsamples n) that automatically logs n decoded predictions alongside evaluation metrics. contribution: i d be happy to work on this feature and open a pr if it s considered useful.']

Name: 18_mask_4d_masks_t5
Representative_Docs: ["looks like at least glm-image doesn't use the masking api argh, yea the vision portions don't use a mask (so pretty much any of the vlms of qwen, zai, and baidu). could we not simply create a mask with in that case? to cover as much as we can, we need to 1. force mask creations where we can, i.e. in the mask api 2. in the sdpa integration, create a zeros mask if mps x affected torch versions x no mask it's a bit of a rubber band tbh, but we don't have much choice. case 1 should cover almost anything and case 2 for the special vlm cases. but wdyt? maybe you have a different idea", "hey i think it would be better to work on integrating creating the new attn mask interface ( instead of reworking on old mask functions, which will be deprecated at some point. i.e. encoder mask and encoder-decoder mask functions similar to the old mask functions ( are less flexible while the new mask function will also be extendable ( to new custom attentions it already allows for passing custom 4d masks ( to answer your questions: - t5 is also quite old, so changing the mask logic there shouldn't affect many models. if there is copied code from t5 (on the parts you modify), you can apply these changes via . some docs are also at (not completely up to date but the utilities are the same). - a test like this would fall under the base t5 model tests, e.g. something like the test should also check for padding to work. - the cross attention mask attention mask, no need to add another argument here. see we should only need to check if that mask was already 4d and ignore any preparation (assumes correctly prepared mask).", 'support for 4d attention mask for t5 feature request currently, t5 cannot take 4d attention masks (batchsize, numheads, seqlen, seqlen) as inputs. passing a 4d attentionmask and a 4d decoderattentionmask like so leads to a shape-related exception : one of the problems in the current code is in the handling of the cross-attention mask. currently, it is created using the 1d encoder attention mask when supplied. however, in the case of a 4d mask, it seems unclear how to correctly use the encoder mask: therefore, the best solution might be to introduce a new 4d mask argument of shape (batchsize, numheads, tgtlen, seqlen) crossattentionmask crossattention mask parameter is the right way to do this and will be approved']

Name: 19_agent_code agent_agents_code
Representative_Docs: ["closing this because it's code agent bait right now!", "hi yes, the issue is real, but we're still closing most code agent prs that show no signs of human review. they're just pointless - the bottleneck for us is reviewing code, and we can run code agents ourselves if we need to, so opening loads of code agent prs just spams the notifications of the maintainers and wastes our time. i'll try to get a proper review for this soon!", "it's actually okay to use agents in your work - we do it all the time! the issue isn't that agents are evil or that users should never use them, it's specifically no-effort agent prs that are the problem. you're welcome to still contribute, but the best ways to be helpful in the agent era are to go beyond what the agents currently do. for example, stuff like this is still very helpful: - not opening prs to other users' issues before checking that they don't want to open a pr themselves. handling that communication and co-ordination well saves work for maintainers. often i have to close 4 different identical agent prs opened on the same issue. - help reviewers out by checking the agent's work more deeply or adding context. for example, look at other models or classes to see how the same issue is handled there, or search for related prs before opening yours. if you're fixing a bug that only appeared recently, do a to identify the pr that introduced the issue. adding this information in the issue or pr body saves time and makes it much easier for the reviewers to verify that your fix is the right one. - adding simple reproducer scripts or tests that show that the fix is working as intended - checking the library more globally rather than just fixing an issue in one place. - checking the ci after opening a pr and fixing failed tests or consistency checks before pinging reviewers code agent prs are often correct! but the problem is that it's not easy for us to verify that they're correct, and we're bottlenecked on reviews and verification now, not on writing code. anything that helps us out with that is greatly appreciated, but issues prs that are just pure code agent work just add more reviewer burden instead; we might as well just eliminate the user and run in our own terminal in that case, it has faster turnaround"]

Name: 20_gemma_gemma4_head_cache
Representative_Docs: ['support sequence classification for gemma 4 models feature request add gemma4forsequenceclassification motivation without this class, fine-tuning gemma 4 on classification tasks requires manually adding a classification head, losing compatibility with automodelforsequenceclassification, trainer, and the standard pipeline workflow. your contribution 45294', "happy to test this against a real downstream use case: qlora fine-tuning gemma 4 e4b on a 3-class sequence classification head. i've got a working local wrapper following the gemma 3 pattern that i can diff against your pr, and i can report back on whether it survives training eval end-to-end.", "add gemma4forsequenceclassification (missing from gemma4 module gemma 2 3 have it) feature request please add gemma4forsequenceclassification (and ideally gemma4textforsequenceclassification ) to transformers.models.gemma4 . the current all in modelinggemma4.py contains only: all gemma4audiomodel , gemma4forcausallm , gemma4forconditionalgeneration , gemma4model , gemma4pretrainedmodel , gemma4textmodel , gemma4visionmodel , no classification head variants are exported, which is inconsistent with every prior gemma release: model family forsequenceclassification textforsequenceclassification -- -- -- gemma n a (text-only) gemma2 n a (text-only) gemma3 gemma3n gemma4 as a result, automodelforsequenceclassification.frompretrained( google gemma-4-e4b , numlabels 3) raises: valueerror: unrecognized configuration class lt;class 'transformers.models.gemma4.configurationgemma4.gemma4config' gt; for this kind of automodel: automodelforsequenceclassification. motivation automodelforsequenceclassification is the standard entry point for fine-tuning causal lms as classifiers a common use case across research (domain classification, sentiment, reward modelling) and production (content moderation, intent detection, financial signal prediction). excluding it from gemma4 forces users to either: roll a custom classifier head wrapper (breaks the automodel contract and duplicates logic that already exists in genericforsequenceclassification), fine-tune generatively and parse argmax over label tokens (worse calibration, loses softmax-head properties), or skip gemma 4 entirely and stay on gemma 3 or a different family. we are currently working around this in a financial-nlp fine-tuning pipeline by subclassing genericforsequenceclassification and registering the result via automodelforsequenceclassification.register(). it works but is exactly the kind of boilerplate the automodel registry is supposed to eliminate, and it has to be duplicated by every project that wants to fine-tune gemma 4 as a classifier. gemma 4 e4b is a mixture-of-experts model (gemma4textexperts gemma4textrouter confirmed in modelinggemma4.py), which makes it particularly inconvenient for users to hand-roll a classification head: the correct pooling behaviour and lastnonpadtoken handling that genericforsequenceclassification provides for free becomes something every user has to re-implement while also navigating moe-specific lora pitfalls. adding gemma4(text)forsequenceclassification upstream would spare every downstream user from reinventing the same wheel. your contribution the implementation should be trivial gemma 3 provides the exact template. in transformers models gemma3 modelinggemma3.py: class gemma3textforsequenceclassification(genericforsequenceclassification, gemma3pretrainedmodel): config: gemma3textconfig inputmodalities ( text ,) and its multimodal sibling gemma3forsequenceclassification, which wires up a gemma3model a nn.linear(textconfig.hiddensize, numlabels) head. the same pattern applied to gemma 4 should require only a few lines of code plus: adding the class(es) to all in modelinggemma4.py registering them in auto modeling_auto.py against gemma4config (and gemma4textconfig if it exists) i'm happy to open a pr if that would help. the change is mechanical but i'd like to confirm first which of the two variants (multimodal-path text-only, or just text-only) the maintainers prefer gemma 3 shipped both, so matching that seems sensible."]

(this is not an invitation for code agents to submit a slop pr to ) in my case, moving the comment before the decorator works. however, i have no idea about the side effects.", "call to contributions: refactor output tracing in transformers following 43590 that updates 112 models, we want to finish migrating all models to the standardized output collection interface. so, this is a call to contributors to open prs to link to this meta-issue and learn about the codebase! two decorators replace the old manual boilerplate: - : goes on the base model forward. automatically collects via hooks on submodules declared in . removes the need for explicit handling. - : goes on wrapper forwards ( , etc.). handles only without hook installation. how to refactor a model 1. add on the subclass. use class references, not strings 2. add on the base model forward (the one with the layer loop) 3. add on higher-level forwards 4. drop , , from signatures 5. remove parameter resolution lines and manual collection loops 6. decoder layers return directly; attention always returns you can use , , or as reference implementations. modern agents are of some help in this issue, but correctly placing the decorators can be tricky. models to update please claim one or several models by commenting before starting. one pr per model (or small group of related models) could be ideal, for instance! as of today here's the remaining list of models: - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -"]

Name: 25_rope_typing_float_int
Representative_Docs: ["ideally should always be because we don't use it anyway. when init the lm backbone, it uses the text config and thus the rope params from text config. in case of qwen2.5vl, we changed the config layout to follow nested structure after model release, and the configs saved in the hub are plain flat dicts. this is the reason that the rope params point to the same object. even though we don't use and discourage its usage, we are keeping it for bc we can fix this by forcefully returning the text config params whenever anything text-related is obtained set, though i am not sure if it is really needed. does trl use directly attribute anywhere?", "improve typing support and flexibility for ropeparameters feature request the new data structure is really improving the clarity of rope configurations across the library. however, i have noticed two areas for improvement: 1. type hints and docstrings for key functions, such as modelingropeutils.standardizeropeparams() ( to clarify and reflect their relationship to the structure. 2. loosening typing enforcement on such that you only need to specify the flags relevant for your model's rope needs by setting in the class definition ( and using the type annotation ( for the few parameters that really do need to to be there. motivation 1. strong typing is almost always better, in my opinion. 2. having to specify the every property is annoying when you only use a couple of them. consider gemma 3. under the current definition of , it requires 28 lines to satisfy the type checker: when this could be expressed in 11 lines: your contribution i'm willing to do all of this. also, i've already been making some progress against these objectives in and", "hey, i think we have to differentiate here between the validation in rope params and validation for other config fields, since we recently added a strict type validation for configs so for rope, i agree that we could just check ( ) and raise warning if not as there are many models in the hub with int values saved. casting shouldn't be needed, the downstream code doesn't crash perform bad with iirc. if you wish, feel free to submit a pr though if you also see an error when loading a config that types do not match (e.g. dropout has to be float but int provided ), it's better to adjust the type hints. we are not currently force-casting to keep type validation light-weight"]

Representative_Docs: ["trainer code is tightly coupled with backend hardware specific calls current version of trainer and its ecosystem of code is tightly coupled with the backend hardware. if someone needs to register their backend to trainer class, then we may need to edit the source code, which is a bad design. code should be extended rather than edited. the original trainer was derived from pytorch-lightning and that had nice device backend registration abstraction. why can't we enforce the same here? below are few references, but i am sure there will be many such references. ref: - - - - - - - -", "probably you didn't pass when initializing the trainer, and it can't save without access to processor. otherwise it is usually saved along with checkpoints this is my processor class: but i don't need it in trainer, will passing this parameter cause any errors? because i noticed that the trainer uses it for some operations.", "tell us: what would make trainer better? rfc: trainer improvements the trainer class is a core component of the transformers library, and we're looking to make it even better. we're gathering inputs on potential improvements, new features, and pain points you've experienced with the trainer class. we're particularly interested in feedback on: - training performance: speed optimizations, memory efficiency, distributed training improvements - api usability: api design, documentation, ease of use, common pain points - features functionality: missing features, callbacks, logging, evaluation, checkpointing - integration: better integration with other libraries or tools - debugging monitoring: better error messages, debugging tools, training diagnostics - developer experience: contributing to trainer, extending functionality, custom trainers questions to guide your feedback for users - what's the most frustrating aspect of using trainer? - what feature would save you the most time? - are there any workarounds you regularly implement that should be built-in? - what use cases does trainer not support well? - how could the documentation be improved? for developers - what would make it easier to extend or customize trainer? - are there architectural changes that would improve maintainability? - what integration points with other libraries should we prioritize? - where do you see technical debt or areas needing refactoring? how to contribute your ideas please share your thoughts by: 1. commenting below with your feature requests or improvement ideas 2. describing your use case - help us understand the problem you're trying to solve 3. being specific - concrete examples and scenarios are extremely helpful 4. upvoting ideas from others that you'd also find valuable what happens next - we'll review all suggestions and feedback - popular and high-impact ideas will be prioritized - contributors interested in implementing features are welcome to volunteer! thank you your feedback drives the evolution of the transformers library. every perspective helps us build better tools for the ml community. special thanks to for getting this conversation started ! list of prs created thanks to this thread surface-level unbloat - : better trainingarguments docstring to easily navigate between the different arguments - : move sort and rotate checkpoints to standalone functions - : move netftune to standalone functions - move optimizers initialization to trainer_optimizers.py file - bunch of minor changes in trainer methods initialization - trainer init - trainingarguments init test reroder - reroder all trainer methods refactor tests - data collator and callbacks - other common trainer tests - distributed tests - template for agents when it comes to testing priority list - soon"]
